title: Rolling a clay slab
subtitle: Producing an even flat sheet of clay for slab-building projects
excerpt: A rolled slab is the foundation of boxes, vases, tiles, and dishes. The method is the same whether the clay is air-dry, paper clay, or kiln-fired stoneware: guide rails to set the thickness, a rolling pin to compress the clay, and a steady hand to keep the slab moving. This tutorial uses air-dry clay and two 6-mm wooden guide rails.
sourceNotes: Slab-rolling technique is a standard hand-building method described in pottery instruction since at least the mid-nineteenth century. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
slab

is a flat sheet of clay pressed and rolled to an even thickness. No kiln, no wheel, and no specialist equipment are needed beyond a rolling pin and two pieces of wood. The technique takes a few minutes to learn and underpins most hand-building forms: boxes, trays, vases, wall tiles, drape-moulded dishes. The guide-rail method removes guesswork about thickness: two wooden strips of the target depth control how far the rolling pin descends.

attrs.body: Work on a smooth, non-porous surface: a sheet of canvas, a plaster bat, or a wooden board. Canvas is ideal because the clay releases cleanly as it dries. Wipe down between sessions. Keep a damp sponge close for rehydrating cracked edges.
attrs.title: Workspace

content.text: What you need

items.name: Canvas board or wooden rolling board
Wooden guide rails, 6 mm thick, at least 25 cm long

content.text: Choosing the clay and thickness

Air-dry clay and paper clay both roll well at room temperature straight from the bag. Cut a block from the bag with the wire cutter rather than tearing it, which creates uneven stress lines in the clay.
Standard thicknesses: 6 mm for wall panels, tiles, and flat pendants; 8 mm for boxes and structural walls; 10 mm for planters that need to support their own weight. The
guide rails
set the thickness: place one rail on each side of the clay block, at the same distance from the centre. The rolling pin spans both rails and cannot press the clay any thinner than the rail height.
Rolling the slab
Step 1: Position the clay between the rails

Place the clay block on the canvas board. Position a guide rail on each side of the clay, parallel to each other and spaced slightly wider than the rolling pin. Press the clay down roughly with the heel of one hand so it is flatter than the guide rails before you begin rolling. This reduces the number of rolling passes needed.
Step 2: Roll from the centre outward

Rest the rolling pin on both guide rails and roll forward from the centre of the clay block toward the far edge. Lift the pin back to centre and roll toward the near edge. Apply firm, even pressure so the clay spreads rather than tears. Do not roll continuously in one direction: rolling only forward folds a ridge into the clay. Alternate forward and back with each pass.
Step 3: Rotate and re-roll

After four to six passes, peel the slab from the canvas by sliding a fettling knife under one edge, then lift and rotate the slab 90 degrees before laying it back down. Rolling only one direction compresses the clay in a single axis and makes it prone to warping when it dries. Re-positioning the rails and rolling in the new direction after each peel keeps the slab uniform.
Step 4: Check the thickness and trim the edges

When the rolling pin contacts both guide rails across the full width of the slab, the rolling is done. The edges of a rolled slab taper slightly and crack; trim them away with the fettling knife, cutting against the guide rail edge for a straight line. Discard the offcuts back into the clay bag, covered with a damp cloth to prevent drying.
What a good slab looks like

Lift one corner: the slab should hold its shape without drooping or tearing. The surface is smooth and slightly shiny with moisture. Press a fingertip into the surface and release: the impression should stay cleanly without the clay cracking or crumbling at the edges. If the edges crack when lifted, the clay is too dry; re-bag and mist with water, then leave for an hour before retrying. If the slab sticks to the canvas and tears when peeled, it is too wet; allow it to firm slightly before trimming.
Drying a rolled slab before use
Most slab-building projects need the slab at
leather-hard

stage rather than straight off the board: at leather-hard, the slab holds a vertical orientation when lifted and does not sag when joined to a corner. Leave the trimmed slab on the canvas board, covered loosely with plastic, for two to four hours at room temperature. Check by lifting one edge: the slab should stand vertically without bending.

For projects where you need the slab to curve (drape-moulded dishes, cylinders), use the slab fresh off the board while it is still pliable. Drape it over or into the mould immediately after trimming.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Problems in slab rolling and how to fix them.

items.fix: Knead the clay block briefly to warm it and re-distribute moisture. If it is too dry to knead without crumbling, wrap in a damp cloth and leave for two hours, then try again.
items.cause: The clay is too dry or too cold.
items.symptom: Slab cracks down the middle when rolled

items.fix: Move the rails closer together so the rolling pin spans both. Roll with the pin pressed firmly down on the rails throughout the stroke.
items.cause: The rolling pin is not contacting both guide rails at the same time, usually because the rails are set too far apart.
items.symptom: Slab is thicker in the centre than at the edges

items.fix: Always rotate the slab 90 degrees between rolling passes. Dry the slab sandwiched between two boards to keep it flat, or cover with a light sheet of plastic to slow drying.
items.cause: Rolling was only in one direction, or one surface dried faster than the other.

items.fix: Allow the slab to firm for 15 minutes before peeling. Slide the fettling knife well underneath before lifting.
items.cause: Too wet, or canvas is not releasing cleanly.
items.symptom: Slab tears when peeled from the canvas

attrs.heading: Common problems
